agent1_snippets.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: The AASM recommends using the term respiratory event index (REI) to differentiate the indices of respiratory events generated by a home sleep study (without recorded sleep). The AHI and REI represent the average number of obstructive events per hour, during sleep or recording time, respectively. Although most portable monitoring devices include flow sensors, other technologies, such as peripheral arterial tonometry (PAT), use alternative methods without flow to identify sleep-disordered breathing events. The severity of OSA obtained using PAT devices is referred to as pAHI, which has been reported to provide indices similar to those derived from PSG-based AHI. [42] The severity of OSA in adults is classified based on AHI, REI, or pAHI as follows: Mild: 5 to 15 events per hour Moderate: Greater than 15 to 30 events per hour Severe: Greater than 30 events per hour

agent1_snippets.title: Sleep Apnea Syndrome -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: The diagnosis of OSA is confirmed on polysomnogram if there are five or more predominantly obstructive respiratory events per hour (which refers to an AHI or RDI 5 or greater) in a patient who has one or more sleep-associated symptoms or conditions (which include sleepiness, fatigue, sleep that is unrestful, insomnia, apneas, gasping or choking, consistent snoring breathing interruptions witnessed by a bed partner, mood disorder, diagnosed hypertension, cognitive dysfunction, cerebrovascular accident, coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation, congestive heart disease, or diabetes mellitus type II). Alternatively, the diagnosis of OSA is also confirmed if there are fifteen or more predominantly obstructive respiratory events per hour (referring to an AHI or RDI of 15 or greater), regardless of associated symptoms or conditions. OSA is generally classified as mild, moderate, or severe. Mild OSA refers to an AHI or RDI between 5 and 14 on a polysomnogram. Moderate OSA refers to an AHI or RDI between 15 and 30. Patients with an AHI or RDI greater than 30 on a polysomnogram are categorized as having severe OSA. [36]

agent1_snippets.title: Sleep Apnea Syndrome -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: The gold standard for the diagnosis of obstructive sleep apnea is an in-laboratory polysomnogram. This is preferred for all patients, but in certain populations/situations, an at-home polysomnogram is an acceptable alternative to an in-laboratory study. Patients in whom an at-home sleep study may be an appropriate alternative include those with no suspicion of another sleep-related diagnosis, an adequate device is available for at-home testing, and a sleep expert is available to interpret the data. If the study is inconclusive or suspicion of OSA remains despite a negative at-home polysomnogram result, an in-laboratory polysomnogram should be completed. [33] [35]

agent1_snippets.title: Sleep Apnea Syndrome -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: An understanding of key indexes of sleep-disordered breathing is required to understand the data of a polysomnogram and diagnose obstructive sleep apnea. The Apnea Index (AI) refers to the total number of apneas per hour of sleep. The Hypopnea Index (HI) is the total number of apneas per hour of sleep. The AI and HI are used to calculate the Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI), which is the total apneas or hypopneas per hour of sleep. This calculation serves as one of the important determinants in the diagnosis of OSA. The RERA Index refers to the total number of RERAs that occur per hour of sleep. The Respiratory Disturbance Index (RDI) is an index that is calculated as the total number of apneas, hypopneas, and RERAs per hour of sleep. The Central Apnea Intex is another useful index calculated by the total number of central apneas per hour of sleep. Similarly, the Mixed Apnea Index may be calculated to find the total number of mixed apneas per hour of sleep.

agent1_snippets.title: Central Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Early detection and diagnosis of CSA can be challenging solely based on self-reported symptoms of disrupted sleep. Nocturnal polysomnography (PSG) is the gold standard diagnostic test in evaluating central apnea is nocturnal polysomnography (PSG). The diagnostic criteria for CSA were published by the American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) in ICSD-3 [2] and varies according to the type of CSA. [17] It generally requires evidence of recurrent central apneas on PSG recording and exclusion of alternative diagnoses. Central apnea is defined as a cessation of flow during sleep for at least 10 seconds without effort (figure 1). [18]

agent1_snippets.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: According to the American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM), hypopnea is defined by either 1 of 2 criteria: A reduction in airflow of at least 30% for more than 10 seconds, accompanied by at least 4% oxygen desaturation (eg, Medicare criteria). A reduction in airflow of at least 30% for more than 10 seconds, associated with either at least 3% oxygen desaturation or an arousal from sleep on EEG (recommended AASM criteria). [39] Scoring apnea requires both of the following criteria to be met: A drop in the peak signal excursion by more than or equal to 90% of the pre-event baseline flow. A duration of the flow reduction of more than or equal to 10 seconds.

agent1_snippets.title: Sleep and Neurodegenerative Disorders -- Evaluation -- Obstructive sleep apnea
agent1_snippets.content: Diagnostic evaluation should be performed on patients with excessive daytime sleepiness, nighttime snoring, or complications associated with sleep apnea. Some of the standard evaluation tools include the STOP-Bang questionnaire and the Epworth Sleepiness Scale. In-laboratory PSG is considered the gold standard test. An HSAT could be considered in patients with a high pretest probability of moderate to severe sleep apnea, suggested by daytime hypersomnolence, habitual snoring, witnessed apnea, gasping, or choking during sleep. agent1_snippets.title: Sleep Apnea Syndrome -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Current recommendations are to obtain diagnostic testing for the workup of OSA in patients with excessive daytime sleepiness on most days as well as two or more of the following symptoms: diagnosed hypertension, loud snoring, witnessed apneas or gasping/choking episodes during sleep. In the absence of these symptoms, some recommend pursuing diagnostic testing for excessive daytime sleepiness alone if there is a concern for obstructive sleep apnea given the overall clinical presentation or if an evaluation for OSA must be ruled out during the workup for another disorder. agent1_snippets.title: Hypoglossal Stimulation Device -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Traditionally, the diagnosis of OSA relied on in-lab polysomnography. However, home sleep apnea testing is now an acceptable alternative diagnostic tool. The results from either of these tests can determine the severity of OSA using the apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) defined by the American Academy of Sleep Medicine. The severity levels are categorized as follows: Mild OSA (AHI 5-15), Moderate OSA (AHI 15-30), and Severe OSA (AHI >30). [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Sleep Apnea Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Alternative and Adjunctive Therapies
agent1_snippets.content: Surgery Intervention is generally not considered first-line therapy for OSA and instead is considered when therapy from positive pressure therapy and/or oral appliance therapy is either ineffective or declines over time. In these patients, identification of the most likely contributing site or sites of obstruction is assessed in a comprehensive exam as above. In addition, some surgeons advocate performing Drug-Induced Sleep Endoscopy (DISE) to further evaluate the airway under anesthetic conditions - however, there is no current consensus on the role of DISE in assessing patients with OSA.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
agent2_snippets.content: Apnea (الاِختِنَاق) is the overarching respiratory disturbance that encompasses the broader category of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ). Within this category, the clinical condition described as respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) represents a more severe manifestation. Thus, adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest are subtypes of the respiratory disorder, which itself is nested under the primary concept of apnea, illustrating a hierarchical relationship that links a fundamental breathing abnormality to increasingly critical respiratory pathologies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
agent2_snippets.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition that reflects an overall increase in arterial pressure. Within the broader vascular category, several specific hypertensive disorders are recognized as subtypes, including أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). Although these entities share the common feature of elevated blood pressure, they differ in their underlying mechanisms, affected vascular territories, and clinical manifestations. Recognizing that elevated mean arterial pressure and its vascular subtypes are interrelated helps clinicians accurately diagnose, monitor, and tailor treatment strategies for patients presenting with various forms of hypertension, thereby improving patient care and outcomes.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1518
agent2_snippets.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia). The condition الاِختِنَاق (apnea) is also mapped to نقص الأكسجين في الدم, linking apnea to the same hypoxemic state seen in sickle cell disease. Together, these relationships underscore that patients with داء الكريات المنجلية are at risk for severe respiratory complications, including hypoxemia and apnea, and highlight the importance of vigilant respiratory monitoring and oxygen management in their care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2895
agent2_snippets.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the broader Vascular category that also encompasses أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (diastolic hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). These entities represent related subtypes of abnormal blood pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a persistently higher average arterial pressure, while hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation that can threaten organ function. Diastolic hypertension is characterized by consistently high diastolic pressure, and systolic hypertension is defined by consistently high systolic pressure. Recognizing that all of these disorders fall under the Vascular umbrella aids clinicians in categorizing blood‑pressure abnormalities, tailoring diagnostic evaluation, and selecting appropriate therapeutic strategies to improve patient outcomes.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936
agent2_snippets.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure, hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), and reactive hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) are all classified within the broader vascular category. These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2757
agent2_snippets.content: Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is a primary form of high blood pressure that frequently presents with an elevated mean arterial pressure as a clinical manifestation. Hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم) is closely linked to essential hypertension, indicating that severe acute blood‑pressure elevations can arise in patients with this condition. Essential hypertension also has a recognized relationship with metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض), reflecting shared pathophysiological mechanisms and overlapping risk factors. Moreover, essential hypertension is commonly mapped to systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقبالي), emphasizing that the systolic component often predominates in its presentation. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians identify the characteristic signs, potential complications, and associated comorbidities of essential hypertension, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2753
agent2_snippets.content: Elevated systolic blood pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the same category as several other high‑pressure disorders. Within the vascular domain, أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) represents an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that requires immediate medical intervention. ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension) denotes a chronic increase in pressure within the pulmonary arteries that occurs without an identifiable secondary cause. فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) describes a pattern in which only the systolic component of arterial pressure is elevated. Together, Elevated systolic blood pressure, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, and systolic hypertension are all considered child entities of the broader Vascular classification, highlighting their shared relevance to vascular pathology and the importance of recognizing and managing these distinct forms of high blood pressure in patient care.
